does: This paper introduces SPACE, the first source-free machine unlearning framework designed for Multimodal Large Language Models that operates without access to target visual data. The method consists of two components: Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS) identifies semantically aligned proxy anchors from the shared vision-language feature space using only text descriptions of concepts to be forgotten, while Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI) optimizes these anchors to erase target concepts while constraining updates to the null space of retained knowledge. The framework theoretically guarantees bounded perturbation on knowledge that should be preserved and demonstrates empirical success at removing sensitive concepts without degrading model performance on retained capabilities.
connects: This work builds on the emerging area of machine unlearning for large models, extending recent approaches that address privacy and regulatory concerns by removing sensitive information post-deployment. It responds to limitations in existing unlearning methods that require direct access to target data, which conflicts with privacy regulations and data retention policies. SPACE combines insights from source-free domain adaptation and concept erasure literature with multimodal learning, leveraging the shared feature spaces that characterize vision-language models like those underlying MLLMs. The null-space constraint approach connects to prior work on catastrophic forgetting mitigation and safe model editing that maintains knowledge integrity during selective removal.
enables: This framework opens pathways for practical deployment of unlearning in real-world MLLM systems where data cannot be retained. It establishes foundations for privacy-preserving machine unlearning without source data access, potentially influencing regulatory compliance strategies across AI systems. The source-free approach could extend beyond visual concepts to other multimodal domains and inspire similar techniques for other large models. Future work might explore dynamic concept erasure, more sophisticated proxy selection strategies, and integration with federated or continual learning settings where models must forget and learn simultaneously. The theoretical bounds on perturbation suggest directions for developing certified unlearning guarantees and more efficient null-space projection methods applicable to increasingly large foundation models.
